options.content:
# W&B Managed Install Performance

Infrastructure health dashboard for a W&B install. Use this dashboard to answer **"is the underlying infrastructure that runs W&B healthy?"** — covers all five managed components (MySQL, Redis, Kafka, SeaweedFS, ClickHouse) plus container resource utilization across the install.

For application-layer signals (W&B API latency, ingest health, slow operations) see the [W&B Field Investigation](/d/wandb-field-investigation) dashboard.

**Quick lookups from the shell:**

- Install size & version: `kubectl get wandb -n <namespace> -o yaml`
- Pod restart reasons: `kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[*].lastState.terminated.reason}{\"\n\"}{end}'`
- OOM-killed events: `kubectl get events -n <namespace> --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | grep -i oom`

Use the **W&B Namespace** dropdown above to switch installs.

### What is this?

Number of times each container in the selected namespace restarted within the last hour.

### Why it matters

A container restarting more than a couple of times per hour is almost always a problem: out-of-memory kill, panic, failed health check, or stuck dependency.

### What to do

- `kubectl get pods -n <namespace>` — note pods with elevated RESTARTS.
- `kubectl describe pod -n <namespace> <pod>` — look at Last State / Reason.
- `kubectl logs -n <namespace> <pod> --previous` — last logs before the restart.

panels.title: Top Containers by CPU (% of limit)

targets.expr: topk(15, sum by (pod, container) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="$namespace", container!="", container!="POD", container!~"agent|aws.*|csi.*|efs.*|external.*|kube.*|liveness.*|prometheus.*|teleport.*|gke.*"}[$__rate_interval])) / on (pod, container) sum by (pod, container) (kube_pod_container_resource_limits{namespace="$namespace", resource="cpu"}))

options.content:
### What is this?

Top 15 containers in the selected namespace by CPU usage as a fraction of the configured CPU limit (1.0 = 100% of limit). Filters out system/agent containers. Includes both W&B services and the managed infrastructure components (MySQL, Redis, Kafka, SeaweedFS, ClickHouse).

### Why it matters

A container sustained above 0.8 (80% of limit) will throttle frequently, slowing every request it handles. Above 1.0 means kernel-side throttling is constant.

### What to do

For the configured limit, run `kubectl describe pod -n <namespace> <pod>` and look at the Limits section, or check `spec.size` in the WeightsAndBiases CR. For sustained saturation, increase the install size.

*Series will be missing for containers without a configured CPU limit.*

panels.title: Top Containers by Memory (% of limit)

targets.expr: topk(15, sum by (pod, container) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="$namespace", container!="", container!="POD", container!~"agent|aws.*|csi.*|efs.*|external.*|kube.*|liveness.*|prometheus.*|teleport.*|gke.*"}) / on (pod, container) sum by (pod, container) (kube_pod_container_resource_limits{namespace="$namespace", resource="memory"}))

options.content:
### What is this?

Top 15 containers in the selected namespace by working-set memory as a fraction of the configured memory limit (1.0 = 100% of limit). Working-set is the memory the kernel will not evict.

### Why it matters

- A line that climbs without leveling off suggests a memory leak.
- A line that touches 1.0 means the container was OOMKilled (visible in the Container Restarts panel above).
- A line consistently above 0.85 means the container is one bad allocation away from OOM.

### What to do

For OOM debug: `kubectl get events -n <namespace> --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | grep -i oom`. For configured limits, see `spec.size` in the WeightsAndBiases CR.

*Series will be missing for containers without a configured memory limit.*

### What is this?

MySQL CPU consumption (fraction of one core) alongside connected and currently-running thread counts.

### Why it matters

- High CPU + high Threads Running = MySQL is the bottleneck. Slow user-facing operations.
- Threads Connected rising while Threads Running stays low = many idle connections, often from a leaked connection pool in an app pod.
- Threads Running near the `max_connections` ceiling = new connections will be refused.

### What to do

For sustained saturation, increase the install size (`spec.size` in the WeightsAndBiases CR), which scales MySQL resources.

### What is this?

Four signals on one panel:

- **questions/sec** — overall query rate hitting MySQL.
- **innodb writes/sec** — write rate, useful for distinguishing read-heavy vs write-heavy load.
- **row lock waits/sec** — rate of new row-lock contention events. A non-zero rate is normal under load; a sustained spike indicates contention from concurrent writes to the same rows.
- **current row lock waits** — point-in-time count of transactions blocked waiting for a row lock. Should be near zero in steady state.

### What to do

Sustained row lock contention usually points to a problematic write pattern. Inspect the MySQL slow query log for the queries involved.

### What is this?

- **aborted clients/sec** — clients that connected and disconnected without properly closing the connection. Often a sign of an app pod restarting under load or a network blip.
- **aborted connects/sec** — connection attempts that failed before reaching the auth handshake. Suggests credentials drift, MySQL refusing connections at the limit, or network-layer issues.
- **slow queries/sec** — queries crossing the `long_query_time` threshold. Sustained non-zero rate is the leading indicator of UI slowness.

### What to do

For sustained slow queries, inspect the MySQL slow query log (`kubectl exec -n <namespace> -it mysql-... -- mysql -e "SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'slow_query%'"` to confirm logging is on, then inspect the file).

### What is this?

- **CPU cores** — sum of user + system CPU consumed by each Redis instance, in cores.
- **memory used** — current memory in use.
- **memory limit** — `maxmemory` config if set; absent (zero/no series) means no maxmemory cap.

### Why it matters

Redis is on the hot path for run-state updates and SDK polling. When CPU saturates or memory approaches the maxmemory cap (and the eviction policy starts evicting), expected reads turn into cache misses, cascading into MySQL load.

### What to do

If eviction is happening (see the **Redis Clients & Eviction** panel below), increase the install size or investigate why the cache is growing faster than expected.

### What is this?

- **connected clients** — live TCP connections to each Redis instance.
- **expired keys/sec** — natural TTL-based expirations. Normal.
- **evicted keys/sec** — forced evictions because `maxmemory` is full. **Any non-zero rate here is a problem** — it means Redis is throwing away cached data the application expected to find.

### What to do

Sustained eviction calls for either raising the Redis memory limit (via install size increase) or investigating why the cache is growing faster than expected. A spike in `expired_keys` without eviction is benign.

### What is this?

- **Messages in by topic** — incoming message rate per Kafka topic, in messages/sec.
- **offline partitions** — partitions that have no leader and can't accept reads or writes. Should always be 0.

### Why it matters

Kafka is the asynchronous backbone for run-state events, parquet-export task scheduling, and other internal queues. When Kafka throughput drops or partitions go offline, downstream consumers stall and user-visible operations slow down.

### What to do

- **Offline partitions > 0**: investigate Kafka broker health immediately. `kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -l strimzi.io/kind=Kafka`, inspect logs, and check that all broker pods are Ready.
- **Sudden drop in messages**: check whether the producer-side services (api, filestream) are healthy in the W&B Field Investigation dashboard.

### What is this?

- **memory tracking** — total bytes ClickHouse is currently using across all queries.
- **HTTP connections** — active HTTP client connections to ClickHouse.
- **merges in progress** — background MergeTree merge operations currently running.

### Why it matters

- **Memory** climbing toward the configured limit signals query-side pressure. ClickHouse aborts queries when memory is exhausted, which surfaces as errors in dependent services.
- **HTTP connections** persistently near zero may indicate clients can't reach ClickHouse.
- **Merges** that climb without coming back down indicate ingest exceeding merge throughput; this leads to part-count limits and eventual write rejections.

### What to do

For sustained memory pressure or merge backlog, increase the install size, which scales ClickHouse resources. For per-query memory diagnostics, query `system.query_log` directly on the ClickHouse pod.

panels.title: Object Store Capacity Used % & Request Rate

options.content:
### What is this?

- **capacity used %** — percentage of usable volume-server capacity in use, derived from `SeaweedFS_volumeServer_resource{type="free"|"all"}`.
- **requests/sec** — filer HTTP request rate across all operations (`SeaweedFS_filer_request_total`).

### Why it matters

SeaweedFS is the object store for parquet files, artifact contents, and run media.

- **Capacity above 90%** = urgent action required. New writes will fail soon.
- **Capacity above 75%** = plan a storage increase.
- **Sudden drop in request rate** = the API or executor services may be unable to reach the filer; check the seaweedfs Service and pod logs.

### What to do

For capacity: increase storage in the WeightsAndBiases CR (`spec.objectStore.managedObjectStore.storageSize`) and re-apply, or migrate older artifacts off-cluster. For request failures: `kubectl logs -n <namespace> -l app.kubernetes.io/managed-by=seaweedfs-operator,app.kubernetes.io/component=filer --tail=200`.

targets.expr: topk(10, histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (service_name, span_name, le) (rate(traces_spanmetrics_duration_milliseconds_bucket{service_name=~"gorilla.*", span_name=~"(?i)(parquet|filestream|filehandler|runstore|historystore|metadatastore|s3|sql).*"}[$__rate_interval]))))

options.content:
### What is this?

p95 latency per storage operation, derived from traces by the OTel `spanmetrics` connector. Covers parquet reads/writes, filestream chunks, S3 calls, and SQL statements.

### Why it matters

Slow chart loads, slow file uploads, and stuck artifact downloads almost always trace back to one storage path being slow. This panel surfaces which one without opening individual traces.

### What to do

- Spikes on `ParquetHistoryStore.*` or `HistoryStore.*` → chart loads will feel slow. Check the parquet pod's CPU/memory in **Container Resource Usage** above, and ClickHouse load if installed.
- Spikes on `FileStreamStore.*` / `FileHandler.*` → ingest stalls. Check filestream container restarts and object store capacity below.
- Spikes on `sql.*` → MySQL is the bottleneck. See **MySQL Errors & Slow Queries** above.
- For per-request detail, [open Traces in Explore](/explore?panes=%7B%22A%22:%7B%22datasource%22:%22${DS_VICTORIATRACES}%22,%22queries%22:[],%22range%22:%7B%22from%22:%22now-1h%22,%22to%22:%22now%22%7D%7D%7D&schemaVersion=1&orgId=1) and filter by the offending `service.name` + `span.name` with `duration > 1s`.
